speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number six with our interview with Mary Stolar, July 13, 1995. OK. Can you just tell you why you wanted to make this video?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, in spite of every-- all the hell what I was through, I am so terribly angry. I am so very angry. And that has inspired what I was truly-- what I was through and what the Jewish people were through in the, in the Holocaust. When I hear this world denying Holocaust, it's driving me up the wall. [SOBS] Soon, I'm going to die. And nobody will ever know what happened really happened. And those anti-Semite-- the neo-Nazis denying this thing, they've got to do more than they are doing. I hope they are going to. And it's terribly important, everybody's testimony and everybody's-- and being still alive and being a very-- unfortunately, a very real witness to this hell. I just can't imagine. I am angry at the whole world and the Jewish people who are in America, I am always angry, angry at them. I am sure they could be somehow-- be helped more than they're helping Bosnia and Herzegovina in Yugoslavia. Nobody was helping us. And we were just left, just to die, like the-- when I see the skeletons at Auschwitz, like the tractors are picking up the skeletons, I say this must be science fiction. I mean, I'm used to it and I know it. I still face the fact in the television. And when I see it, it's like science fiction. Those are skeletons. They are people who were alive a year ago, a few years ago, children lying-- I mean, I don't know if you saw the piles they're picking up with these tractors in the, in the-- It's-- it's like absolutely not, not real. And I definitely, in spite of it makes me very depressed. I am depressed enough. The memory and it's what I testified, it's, it's to-- it's kind, kind insured. Because there's so many-- every little episode, every little detail is one pure hell. And what I can-- and what I can, it's perhaps things-- small things will come back to me. It's more-- more to it. Every, every episode, every visit when this alcoholic, when this scholar, they always beat us. Once, I'll never forget when my son wake up in the morning. Because it was in the evening when he beat us up, and we were screaming. And, and I was holding and I fall asleep. In the morning, he got the nose so swollen.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I wrote my biography, when I came to be part where my son was suffering, asking, Mama, can I have a tiny little bread? I'm so hungry, please, Mom. After the war he was-- they sent him away to a sanatorium. He was tuberculosis. Thank God he went out of it. And he's so bulky and things and look what happened to him, 11 years ago, 11 and 1/2.

speaker: interviewee
text: He neglected it with the urine. And he neglected. He went-- having trouble urinating. And this is very dangerous for a man, right away the prostate.

speaker: interviewer
text: And doctor, me, a doctor? What do you need to go for a doctor for? It is intellect with everything he's neglected it and that's how. OK, we're going to look at the pictures now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: You want me to remove this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait a second.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm not going to talk anymore, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: One second.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was about 28 years old. It was in [INAUDIBLE]. No, I am sorry. This was already in Israel, in Israel. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, must have been shot before we left. It was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, shot after we came to Israel. It was, must have been in 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a picture made in-- also in Israel, a year later. Must have been, I must have been 30 years old. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 1952, must have been, '51-- do you think I remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture I made with my son, before he finished the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was he with?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I can't recall the, the, the exact date. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, it's you and your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, myself and my son. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This picture is of, as you can see, myself and my son. This is a picture of my son. He must have been in the early teens, about 14, 15-year-old, in Israel. This is a picture of my son, who started in the army, out in the army, in his uniform, as paratrooper, as a paratrooper in the air force. This is a picture of myself and my son in Switzerland. I look here like I've gained a few pounds. [LAUGHS] My son used to live in Switzerland for two years. And I visit him over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Must have been in 19-- this must have been in 1967 or '68. This is a picture of my two, two, dearly grandchildren, a girl and a boy. The girl's name is [INAUDIBLE]. And she's at the present nine-- nine and a half years old. This little boy's name is Daniel. He's in the present five and a half years old. And they both, of course, live in Israel with their parents. This is a picture of my granddaughter, when she-- I don't know exactly how old she was here, the firstborn, with my son, with her father, who she adores and he adores her in Israel.
